⚠ Figure failed to load

plan.contextChunk:
can use the equation of a line in homogeneous coordinates,
$\boldsymbol{l}^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{p}=0$. From equation
(@eq-homography_generic), we have that
$\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{H}^{-1} \mathbf{p}'$. Replacing the last equality
into the equation of the line gives
$(\mathbf{H} ^{-\mathsf{T}}\boldsymbol{l})^\mathsf{T}\mathbf{p'}=0$,
which corresponds to the equation of the line for the projected points
$\mathbf{p}'$.

In a general homography, angles and lengths are not preserved.
Therefore, parallel lines might not remain parallel, and lines of
identical length might have different lengths after a homography.
@fig-illustration_homography2 shows how a planar grid gets transformed
after applying a homography.

![Homography. Colinear points remain colinear after transformation. Angles between lines are not preserved.](figures/homography/example_homography.png){width="70%" #fig-illustration_homography2}

Let's start discussing in which scenarios the coordinates of points
between to images are related by a homography.

### Camera Rotation

Consider the following three images shown in @fig-example_three_images.

:::{#fig-example_three_images layout-ncol="3"}
![](figures/homography/3_IMG_7883_small.jpg){width="52%"}

![](figures/homography/2_IMG_7884_small.jpg){width="30%"}

![](figures/homography/1_IMG_7885_small.jpg){width="30%"}

interactions.label: Warp Corners
interactions.teaches: Dragging any of the four corner handles directly alters the 3x3 homography matrix, showing how 4 point correspondences uniquely define a planar projective transformation.

interactions.label: Interaction Mode
interactions.teaches: Switches between manual 2D corner manipulation and a physical 3D scene that demonstrates how camera rotation/tilt physically causes this homography.

interactions.label: Tilt Pitch
interactions.teaches: Tilts the grid vertically relative to the camera, causing horizontal grid lines to converge to a horizontal vanishing line.

interactions.label: Tilt Yaw
interactions.teaches: Rotates the grid horizontally, creating a strong perspective foreshortening towards the right just like in the original textbook figure.

demo_steps.title: Colinearity is preserved
demo_steps.narration: The default view replicates the textbook figure. Observe that while the square grid on the left is heavily distorted on the right, every grid line remains perfectly straight. Under any homography, colinear points always map to colinear points.

demo_steps.title: 8 degrees of freedom
demo_steps.narration: Drag the four green handles on the right grid. Because a homography is defined up to a scale factor, it has exactly 8 independent parameters (degrees of freedom), meaning that positioning these 4 corners completely determines the mapping of the entire grid interior.

demo_steps.title: Camera rotation physical origin
demo_steps.narration: Switch to '3D Camera Tilt' mode. Now, the warp is physically generated by tilting the grid plane relative to a virtual camera. As you adjust the Pitch and Yaw sliders, you can see how perspective projection naturally produces the vanishing lines and foreshortening.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Front-facing 2D orthographic canvas displaying the source grid on the left, an arrow in the middle, and the warped grid on the right.

camera_view.view_notes: The original figure consists of two side-by-side flat panels separated by an arrow. The primary view must align perfectly with this layout to act as a seamless replacement.

interactionPlan.notes: In 'Corner Drag' mode, the homography matrix H is solved using the direct linear transform (DLT) algorithm for the 4 corner points, and all interior grid intersections are mapped as H * x. In '3D Camera Tilt' mode, H is computed directly from the rotation matrix R and camera intrinsics K, showing the mathematical equivalence.

attempts.html:
drag green corners
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The left grid is severely clipped/cut off on the left edge of the screen
Only two green corner handles (left-top and left-bottom) are visible on the right grid, while the right-side ones are missing
The UI controls (Mode, Pitch, Yaw sliders) directly overlap the left grid geometry, causing clutter and visual disruption
Green corner handles are visible on the right grid by default, whereas the original textbook figure is clean and contains no colored handles
evaluation.notes: The left grid is severely cut off and the UI controls overlap the interactive area, though the homography math is solid.
action_items: Adjust the orthographic camera zoom and target so that the left grid is fully framed and not clipped on the left edge
Reposition the HTML UI elements (select dropdown, sliders) into a clean, non-overlapping side panel or corner overlay so they do not cover the grid geometry
Investigate and fix why the right-side green corner handles are missing/clipped from the right grid in Corner Drag mode
Hide the green interactive handles by default or match the clean, unannotated look of the original textbook image on first load

feedback.rationale: The conceptual homography math is perfect (concept accuracy 5), but the execution has layout, clipping, and UI styling issues that require implementation-level refinement.
action_items: Adjust the orthographic camera zoom and target so that the left grid is fully framed and not clipped on the left edge
Reposition the HTML UI elements (select dropdown, sliders) into a clean, non-overlapping side panel or corner overlay so they do not cover the grid geometry
Investigate and fix why the right-side green corner handles are missing/clipped from the right grid in Corner Drag mode
Hide the green interactive handles by default or match the clean, unannotated look of the original textbook image on first load
actionItems: Adjust the orthographic camera zoom and target so that the left grid is fully framed and not clipped on the left edge

Hide the green interactive handles by default or match the clean, unannotated look of the original textbook image on first load

attempts.html:
drag green corners
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Green circular drag handles are present on the corners of the warped grid, which do not exist in the original static image.
Text labels 'original grid', 'homography warp', and the equation 'p′ = H p' are added to the visualization, whereas the original has only the grids and an arrow.
The arrow in the middle has the equation 'p′ = H p' placed above it, which is not in the original.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive recreation of the homography warp with highly educational corner-dragging and 3D camera simulation controls.
action_items: Add an option to toggle or hide the green corner handles to perfectly match the pristine clean look of the original figure.
Allow the user to hide the explanatory text labels ('original grid', 'homography warp', 'p′ = H p') to achieve 100% visual faithfulness to the source book image.
Ensure the UI controls at the bottom are slightly more compact or collapsible to minimize coverage of the main illustration area on smaller screens.

feedback.rationale: The figure is excellent with nearly perfect scores, outstanding interactive functionality, and only minor stylistic differences that actually enhance the educational value of the interactive 3D version.
action_items: Add an option to toggle or hide the green corner handles to perfectly match the pristine clean look of the original figure.
Allow the user to hide the explanatory text labels ('original grid', 'homography warp', 'p′ = H p') to achieve 100% visual faithfulness to the source book image.
Ensure the UI controls at the bottom are slightly more compact or collapsible to minimize coverage of the main illustration area on smaller screens.
